text: limit when someone dropped off a malnourished runt named Biscuit a few weeks after lockdown, and as the kitten devoured can after can of $2.35 gourmet sustainable organic tuna paté, she couldn’t help laughing at her bad luck. She does that a lot these days. Instead of facing reality, she pretends she’s the main character in a madcap sitcom, or the heel on a hidden camera prank show. Really hamming it up, she exaggerates every guffaw and eye roll and dances with the house-cats to riotous cheers from the audience in the shadows. Everything had been so unbelievably crazy for so long, it wasn’t difficult to convince herself she was being watched. Or that it was practice for all the interviews she’ll be doing once lockdown ended. She just needs to stick to the plan. Only the oldest house-cats remember how hard-won Lisa’s dream was. All those years planning and perfecting her vision for the cat cafe and proving to Charlie his money wasn’t going to waste—not that he cared all that much about money. He just wanted his young bride to be happy; the hows and whys of getting there didn’t matter. Frankly, that kind of attitude infuriated her. The path to achieving one’s dream was just as important as the dream itself, and every messy step deserved to be acknowledged, if not honored. Before they married, he warned her he wasn’t the type to get worked up about such things, even when he was in his prime, and after retirement, it was likely to get worse. But Lisa firmly believed the right woman could kindle even the lowest burning fire to a blaze. The right woman could also smother it. Charlie would’ve loved lockdown. The silence, the darkness. He was the kind of man who could stare out the window for hours, perfectly content, or read the same damn biography of Thomas Jefferson twice a year and still marvel at something new he’d learned. He didn't want dreams or aspirations or even hobbies that required much effort. Every day with breath in his lungs was enough for him, and having a beautiful dreamer for a wife to boot was, as he put it, “More than enough.” Lisa hated that. It sounded like a compliment, but it wasn't. If “enough” made him “perfectly content,” then “more than enough” had to feel overwhelming. “Don’t you ever get tired of me?” she asked more than once. “Don’t you ever wish I would stop asking you for things every second of every day?” He shrugged. “Not really. I like giving you things.” “But don’t you ever see what I’m doing and think, ‘Gosh, I could be out there helping people too. I could be making friends or traveling the world’. Don’t you ever get tired of just sitting around this house?” “Not really. I like this house. I like the cafe. And I love you.” “There must be something you’ve always dreamed of doing.” He smiled. “Your dreams are my dreams. And vice versa.” Even the memory made Lisa’s face tighten in irritation. Their dreams were never interchangeable. His were boring, rudderless things, like the mushrooms growing in the cracks of the cellar floor. Hers changed lives. On the surface, Ninth Life was a cute little cafe where someone could grab a homemade muffin while playing with foster cats, but at its core, the cafe connected folks who longed for companionship with animals that longed for anything . And how was Lisa Willis repaid for this valuable service? Squinting at the cobwebbed cafe, she hisses, “Non-essential.” A hushed gasp rises from the studio audience, and she limps back into the house. They’re getting harder to shake. Even when she’s not trying to pretend, she feels like she’s being watched. She’s never seen anyone looking back from the dark, but they’re real enough in her mind. Their eyes, gray lidless orbs, are sunken and off-set, like God plopped them into place while the flesh was still drying. And when they applaud her endeavors, success or failure, exposed bones clatter against one another, and lumps of withered flesh drop to the floor like overripe plums. Thankfully she's practical enough to recognize it’s just the stresses of quarantine playing tricks, so whenever visions of the dead audience frighten her, she throws her head back and chuckles uproariously for the camera. It is pretty funny how powerful her imagination can be. In reality, the only thing watching from the shadows is Frankie, the cat that refuses to purr. Her old tabby Cornelius wobbles into the foyer with a creaky mew and bumps his head on her shin. She scoops him up and presses her lips to his crown, giving him a squeaky kiss that summons more cats. She needs them badly right now, or she’ll never make it through the next step of the plan. Unfortunately the sound also calls Frankie, who hasn’t gotten the hint that her kisses aren’t for him. Not until he gives her what she needs. Cornelius mews again, this time in pain. She’s squeezing him too hard and he’s trying to escape. When she loosens her grip, he bounds out of her arms and gallops down the hall, past the grandfather clock, where Frankie sits, chin raised and tail slowly swishing. She hisses, “Non-essential,” and Frankie’s eyes widen. He sniffs the air, and as if mocking her with promises of fidelity, blinks in that sweet, slow way she associates with affection. It’s not real though. She’s lived with this mongrel long enough to know his blinks are a tease. Still, deep-down, from a place she can neither tap nor staunch, she feels a twinge of hope at every blink. She hates herself for that. It’s too familiar a cycle to repeat, even with a cat. Especially with a cat. Cats are supposed to understand you when humans don't. They’re supposed to love you unconditionally and make you feel like the cruelty and coldness of men can’t touch you. Cats and their
